subject: English Language Arts

secondary_architecture_support.note: A condensed single-period adaptation is possible, but the two-day sequence remains primary because students need planning, checkpoint, and final writing stages.

materials_control_note: Planning materials and checkpoint materials remain teacher-controlled until the final response release point.

lesson_overview.overview: Students explore a familiar school issue across two class days, study a short evidence set, gather reasons and evidence in a task sheet, complete a teacher checkpoint, and then write a final argument paragraph in the declared final evidence location.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How can we use reasons and evidence to write a clear argument about a community issue?

sequence.focus: Introduce the issue, study the evidence set, gather reasons and evidence, and prepare for the teacher checkpoint.
sequence.carryover: Student planning from the task sheet continues into Day 2.

sequence.focus: Re-open planning, strengthen explanation, and write the final argument paragraph in the final response sheet.
sequence.carryover: Day 1 planning stays available while students draft their final response.

integrity_checks: Teacher-only checkpoint guidance stays in teacher-facing outputs.
Student planning work stays in the task sheet until the final response release point.
Final evidence stays in the final response sheet, not in teacher materials or slide notes.

teacher_guide.big_idea: Argument writing becomes clearer when students gather reasons and evidence before drafting, explain how evidence supports a reason, use a checkpoint to refine their thinking, and keep final evidence in a declared student-facing artifact.
learning_goals: I can guide students through a two-day argument-writing sequence without collapsing planning and final evidence into the same artifact.
I can use a checkpoint to strengthen student reasoning before final writing.
I can help students explain how evidence supports a reason, not just list information.
I can keep final written evidence in the declared final response location.

timing.activity: Launch the community issue and clarify the purpose of the planning task sheet.
Read the evidence set and discuss which evidence seems strongest.
Students gather reasons, evidence, and explanation in the task sheet.
Teacher uses the checkpoint sheet to confirm students are ready for final writing.
Students revisit Day 1 planning, compare weak and strong explanation, and strengthen one part of their argument.
Students draft the final argument paragraph in the final response sheet.

teacher_notes: Do not release the final response sheet on Day 1.
Keep sentence starters and planning supports embedded in the student-facing artifacts.
Use the checkpoint to push students beyond opinion toward stronger reason-and-evidence connections.
likely_misconceptions: Students list a reason but do not explain why the evidence supports it.
Students copy a detail from the evidence set without linking it to their claim.
look_fors: Student can state a clear opinion about the issue.
Student can identify at least two usable reasons.
Student can point to a piece of evidence connected to a reason.
Student can explain why the evidence supports the reason, not just repeat it.
support_moves: Ask: Which reason do you believe most strongly? Say it in one sentence before writing.
Ask: Which fact or detail from the evidence set helps prove that reason?
Use the frame: This evidence matters because ___.
extension_moves: Invite stronger writers to acknowledge another perspective before reinforcing their own claim.
Invite stronger writers to revise for more precise argument language instead of repeating opinion words.

slides.title: Writing About a Community Issue — Day 1

content.subtitle: Launch the issue, study the evidence set, and plan your argument.

content.scenario: A school is deciding whether to make lunch periods longer. Some students say it would improve focus and reduce stress. Others say it would create scheduling problems. How could we build a clear argument about this issue?
prompts: What opinion might someone have about this issue?
What reasons might support that opinion?
What evidence would make the argument stronger?

columns.title: Evidence students can use
items: Student survey: 68% of students said they feel rushed during lunch.
Teacher note from school council: Longer lunch could reduce time available for electives or support blocks.
Student wellness club feedback: Some students say a calmer lunch break helps them return to class more focused.

columns.title: Think like a writer
items: Which detail supports a longer lunch?
Which detail supports a shorter lunch or current schedule?
Which detail would need explanation to become strong evidence?

slides.title: Worked model: claim + reason + evidence + explanation

content.model: Claim: Our school should make lunch periods longer. Reason: A longer lunch would help students feel less rushed and more ready to learn. Evidence: In a student survey, 68% of students said they feel rushed during lunch. Explanation: This evidence matters because if many students feel rushed, they may return to class distracted or stressed instead of ready to focus.
supports: Notice that the explanation tells why the evidence supports the reason.
The writer does more than repeat the fact.
Use this structure in your own planning.

content.task: Use your task sheet to plan your argument so you can carry strong reasoning into Day 2.
prompts: Which evidence from the evidence set supports that reason?

task_sheet.title: Day 1 Task Sheet
instructions: Record reasons and evidence you can use again on Day 2.
Explain how the evidence supports your reason, not just what the evidence says.

tasks.label: Part A
tasks.prompt: Write the community or school issue in your own words and state your opinion clearly.

tasks.label: Part B
tasks.prompt: Record two reasons that support your opinion.

tasks.label: Part C
tasks.prompt: Choose at least one detail from the evidence set for each reason.

tasks.label: Part D
tasks.prompt: Explain: How does this evidence support your reason?

tasks.label: Part E
tasks.prompt: List one question or weak spot you still need to improve before writing your paragraph.

embedded_supports: Sentence starter: One important reason is ___.
Sentence starter: This evidence matters because ___.
Box the reason you think is strongest before the checkpoint.
success_criteria: I collected reasons and evidence I can use tomorrow.
I explained how my evidence supports a reason.
I kept my planning in the declared task-sheet location.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Day 1 Checkpoint Sheet
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Teacher checkpoint before Day 2 final response release.
look_fors: Student has at least two usable reasons.
Student can point to evidence or explanation for those reasons.
Student can explain how the evidence supports the reason.
conference_prompts: Show me the strongest reason in your planning.
What evidence makes that reason believable?
How does that evidence support your reason?
What still needs strengthening before you draft tomorrow?
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Release the Day 2 final response sheet only when the student can point to usable reasons and evidence in the task sheet and explain at least one connection between evidence and reason.

days.carryover_note: Use Day 1 planning before drafting the final argument paragraph.

slides.title: Writing About a Community Issue — Day 2

content.subtitle: Revisit your planning, strengthen your explanation, and write your final paragraph.

content.scenario: Before you draft, look back at the reasons and evidence you carried from Day 1.
prompts: Which piece of evidence needs clearer explanation?
How will you organize your final paragraph?

content.left: Weak: Longer lunch is better because 68% of students feel rushed.
content.right: Strong: Longer lunch is better because 68% of students feel rushed during lunch, which suggests many students may return to class distracted or stressed instead of ready to focus.
prompts: Which version explains the evidence more clearly?
What words or phrases make the stronger version better?
How can you improve your own explanation before drafting?

content.task: Strengthen one reason or one piece of evidence in the task sheet before you move to the final response sheet.
prompts: Make sure your final paragraph will stay in the final response sheet only.
Which reason made your argument strongest?
What changed between your planning and your final paragraph?
How did explanation improve your argument?

task_sheet.title: Day 2 Task Sheet
instructions: Re-read your Day 1 planning before making changes.
Strengthen one reason or one piece of evidence.
Improve your explanation before drafting the final paragraph.

tasks.label: Part A
tasks.prompt: Review your strongest reason and explain why you are keeping it in your final paragraph.

tasks.label: Part B
tasks.prompt: Revise one piece of evidence or explanation so it supports your opinion more clearly.

tasks.label: Part C
tasks.prompt: Write one improved explanation sentence using the frame: This evidence matters because ___.

embedded_supports: Underline the reason you will lead with in your final paragraph.
Keep planning work separate from your final paragraph.
success_criteria: I improved the planning I started on Day 1.
I know which reasons and evidence belong in my final paragraph.
I improved at least one explanation before drafting.
I kept planning separate from final evidence.

final_response_sheet.title: Day 2 Final Response Sheet
final_response_sheet.prompt: Write one argument paragraph that states your opinion clearly, gives reasons, and uses evidence or explanation to support your thinking.
planning_reminders: Use one or two strong reasons from your task sheet.
Explain why your evidence supports your argument.
Keep your final written answer on this sheet.
success_criteria: My paragraph states a clear opinion.
My paragraph uses reasons and supporting evidence.
My paragraph explains why the evidence supports my argument.
My final evidence is recorded in the declared final response location.
